lesson.menu_title: Block 3 -- Making the Business Case: Justifying the Investment
lesson.menu_desc: Make the business case for the next phase: compare the options (build vs buy, this vendor vs that), and justify which one delivers the most value -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- Block 3 -- Making the Business Case: Justifying the Investment
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Carlos Vinicius Vale Bassan | Aula 14 -- Making the Business Case

listenings.text: Carlos, before the board asks you to justify the spend, let me brief you on the two options for the next phase. Option one is to build the platform in-house. The upfront cost is lower, and it is more flexible than a vendor product, because the team can shape it. But the payback period is longer, the lead time is the biggest risk, and it is harder to scale. Option two is to buy from the vendor. It is more expensive upfront, but it is faster, it is more scalable, and the return on investment is higher over three years, because the payback is quicker. So the honest comparison is this: building is cheaper to start but slower to pay back; buying costs more now but delivers the most value over the cycle. The downside of building is the time; the downside of buying is the price tag the board will see first. My advice: lead with the option that is the most cost-effective over three years, not the one that is the cheapest on day one. Show the numbers side by side, name the trade-off, and recommend the one that is more viable for where we want to be. Boards approve the spend when the case is clear, not when it is loud.

Hi Carlos, this is Diana, with a quick recap after the investment review. The directors liked the way you made the case. You compared the two options side by side, you were clear that buying is more expensive upfront but more cost-effective over three years, and you did not hide the downside of either path, which they respected. A few notes for next time. First, the payback period: that was your strongest number, so lead with it, because a shorter payback is the most persuasive point for this board. Second, scalability: you said the vendor option is more scalable, but the board wanted proof, so bring one concrete example of how it scales. Third, the upfront cost: do not apologize for it; frame it as the price of the higher return. One thing to add: a single slide that shows the return on investment of each option as one clear number, because the board decides faster when the comparison is one line, not ten. Send me the updated business case by Wednesday, and we will walk the lead director through it before the vote. Strong work, Carlos. You made the more expensive option sound like the smarter one, and that is exactly the case we needed. Talk soon.
